data.title: Netflix Earnings Preview: Q3 2025
data.description:
Netflix Inc. (NFLX) will report third quarter 2025 results on Tuesday, Oct. 21, 2025. Here are the key numbers that we're watching.

The preview

Expectations have remained stable throughout the third quarter. In the previous three-month period, the company provided guidance for a stable outlook, in line with consensus. Visible Alpha consensus expects $11.5 billion in revenue for the third quarter and $45.1 billion for the full year 2025. Revenue is expected to be supported by continued new membership and monetization. Offering a range of pricing and plans, combined with continuing growth in the ads business, is expected to further increase monetization. Consensus expects the operating margin to increase to 31.5% in the third quarter and to 30.3% for full year 2025.

The company expects to grow revenue by increasing engagement trends and reducing churn while offering more diverse entertainment products. Gaming and the growth of ads could be key drivers in 2025. According to consensus, analysts now expect the company to generate a 30.3% margin, up from 29.6% in previous estimates, on expected revenue of $45.1 billion and $13.6 billion in operating profit in full year 2025.

In the spring, management highlighted that the Ad Tier enables lower prices. The company stated in the earnings call that they expect ad revenue to roughly double year over year again in 2025. Since early 2025, the ad-supported revenue estimate has been moving higher into the third quarter earnings release. While expectations are still below the initial forecast of $1.6 billion back in early 2024, the trend is improving and is now at $1.1 billion.

Netflix remains upbeat about the long-term opportunity, given the size of its user base. Co-CEO Gregory Peters explained that 2025 will be the year that the ads business will step up to the plate to swing. Currently, consensus projects total ad-supported revenue for full year 2027 to expand to $6.5 billion, but down from the expected $7.4 billion in 2024. There is a significant range of views on the magnitude of growth. For full year 2027, the analyst estimates range has widened from $3.5 billion to $9.2 billion to between $3.8 billion and $13.8 billion.

Based on Visible Alpha consensus, the operating profit margin is expected to grow from 26.7% in full year 2024 to 35.1% in full year 2027, an increase of 70 basis points since last quarter. Currently, consensus estimates the operating margin to exceed 32% by the end of 2026. There is significant debate among analysts with respect to full-year 2027 margin estimates, which range from 32% to 37%. This margin growth is expected to take full-year 2024 expected diluted EPS from $20.22 to $39.77, or 30x the full-year 2027 P/E, significantly higher than the 25x it traded in spring. The current consensus target price has ticked up to $1,400, or nearly 17% upside from the current levels.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ethan Allen Interiors: Target Price Is Justified By Strategic Operations And Valuation
data.description:
It’s been three months since my first analysis of Ethan Allen Interiors, Inc. (NYSE:ETD). And after a 6.2% stock price decrease, my hold rating was justified. I also think it helped that my projected revenue of $614M was close to the reported amount of $614.6M at the end of the year. Right now, ETD still faces several challenges. Yet, its solid Balance Sheet and new market opportunities may help boost or at least stabilize its performance in FY 2026. Valuation still shows underpricing, but the question about whether or not an upside is possible should be considered again. Technicals still show bullish signals, just what I expected before, but downside risks are there.

Challenges

Inflation remains a primary challenge for businesses within the consumer discretionary sector. Ethan Allen Interiors, Inc. remains vulnerable to risks as inflation continues to affect pricing flexibility, demand, and cost pressures. That is why its Q4 2025 performance turned out to be in line with my revenue expectations. Its operating revenue amounted to $160.4M, down by -4.9% YoY from $168.6M. This brought sales to a total of $614.6M. Reduced production volume and units delivered remained the primary challenge it faced during the quarter. Even now, I believe ETD still experiences this to manage the impact of inflation. Note that the Fed estimates that inflation climbed to over 3.0% last month. This would be the first time that inflation would reach this level after seven months. The trend may continue as the Fed expects that the target of 2.0% won’t be met until 2028. With that, discretionary spending may still be soft in H1 2026.

Another challenge is the weak housing market. While the median home sales have already gone down to $410,800, pre-pandemic levels are still much lower. Although it is 2% lower than in my previous coverage, the price is still above $400,000. This continues to soften housing demand, which, in turn, also softens the demand for new home furnishings and decorations.

Considering all this, I’m keeping my 2026 estimates the same as in my previous coverage. Another revenue drop of -5% makes sense for me. From the most recent revenue, it may decrease to $584M this FY. And since I set COGS and revenue as a percentage of revenue, the operating profit will decrease in the same way. To have a broader view, I also estimated depreciation and capex using the straight-line method to compute NOPAT and EBITDA. The consolation is that I expect ETD to remain profitable as it stays prudent with its cost and inventory management.

Drivers/Mitigants

Despite the challenges it faces today, ETD may benefit from some favorable market changes.

1.Interest Rate Cuts And Decreasing Mortgage Rates Can Bring Some Positive Spillovers. Last month, the Fed decided to cut the interest rate by 25 bps to 4.25% after keeping it untouched for 10 months. This move aims to boost labor market conditions. And if this succeeds, higher employment and wages may also take place. I think this can help partially offset the impact of inflation and increase confidence among consumers and borrowers. As a result, discretionary spending may increase. And I think this is timely for home repairs or renovations ahead of the holiday season and winter.

On top of that, interest rate cuts can lower adjustable-rate mortgages or ARMs and HELOCs. It can also lower 30-year mortgage rates since these are directly tied to the 10-year treasury yields. The yield typically drops with the interest rate. As you can see, the 30-year mortgage rate is approaching 6% again after staying at/near 7% for quite some time. These changes could possibly entice homeowners and raise the demand for ETD’s products.

2.Strategic Location Makes ETD More Competitive. Many companies in the home furnishings industry are vulnerable to new tariffs due to their inflationary nature. This is especially risky for those heavily reliant on product and raw material imports. That may not be the case for ETD due to its strong domestic production footprint in the US and Honduras. As I explained in my previous article, Honduras has more tariff insulation. As you can see, most countries face new tariffs of at least 15%. The remaining ones, including Honduras, pay 10%. This means that ETD could possibly manage cost pressures than its competitors who source products from countries with higher tariffs.

Moreover, Honduras is among those countries with low labor costs or monthly wages. I look up various sources on the Internet. The Global Living Wage Org estimated that the average monthly wage in Honduras was only $277. But since several years have already passed since its publication, I looked for other sources. According to Playroll, the average monthly wage in 2025 is $620, but this may vary. For microbusinesses, the average is only about $370. In small and medium businesses, wages are about $370 and $550, respectively. And for large companies, this can reach $860. Either way, wages in Honduras are cheap, so ETD enjoys lower labor expenses.

As we combine the impact of lower tariffs and labor expenses in Honduras, ETD may be more competitive in pricing and marketing strategies without sacrificing sales and profits. And I think this still works to its advantage despite lower sales last quarter. In fact, written orders for the retail segment increased by 1.6%. In addition, its operating leverage decreased from 45.1% to 44.3%, which means that ETD is less cost-sensitive as of this writing. While this was not enough to offset the sales decrease, it helped ETD maintain positive earnings.

3.Strong Liquidity Means Business Sustainability And Dividend Longevity. ETD remains efficient to ensure positive profits. One important aspect is its prudent inventory management. The decreasing inventory level in its Balance Sheet is consistent with its demand. That way it can optimize its production and manage costs and expenses. It still works to its advantage as it remains profitable with positive cash inflows.

As we can see, its operating cash flow has stayed adequate to cover capex and dividends in the past 11 quarters. So, it’s not surprising that its cash level also remains stable above $100M. It doesn’t have debts, which means that ETD generates enough earnings and/or cash inflows to sustain its operations and dividend payouts on its own. Hence, it has enough capital buffer to ensure its liquidity.

Valuation

As I revisit the stock, I notice its price appears to be most reactive with book value per share, not EPS nor sales per share. With that, I decided to use P/B to assess it. As you can see, the stock trades at 1.48x its book value versus the five-year average of 1.50x. If we use it to derive the fair value of ETD, the target price, or TP, will be $28.45. This shows that ETD trades at a reasonable price. It is neither cheap nor expensive relative to its FY 2021-2025 average.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. Note that I based its annualized dividends of $1.56 on the quarterly regular payout of $0.39. Its dividend growth rate is the average growth rate since 2021 to exclude the impact of the pandemic. Meanwhile, I estimated its cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. Note that I set its risk premium at 6.0%, which is slightly above the standard rate of 5.0% to price in more risks. Lastly, I estimated its Beta using the SLOPE function on the daily price change of the stock versus the S&P 500 on MS Excel. Using all these numbers, I derived a TP of $31.39. If I add a 5% risk consideration, the TP will be $29.81. This shows some upside on the stock price.

Technicals

ETD’s trend has weakened in the past two weeks following its consolidation for three months. The price has already crossed below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that the previous uptrend after the correction may not hold. Right now, the price is still consolidating despite its slight dip. The 50-day SMA is still flattish above the 200-day SMA. This shows that market sentiments are still neutral and that there is still a chance that the price will increase again.

Its MACD and RSI explain the logic behind the recent trend. The MACD line is still below the signal, which shows that the selling volume is not over yet. It is also within negative territory along with the histogram, which means that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. But it is worth noting that the histogram slightly improved in the past three trading days. And as you can see, the stock had net buying for two consecutive days. This can tell us that buyers found new support at $27. RSI also shows recent overselling that prompted reentries. At this point, it is already within normal range. The buying strength index may not be as strong as it was two days ago. But it is still closer to the oversold region, so there's still some room to push the price up again.

Conclusion

Ethan Allen Interiors, Inc. experiences macroeconomic challenges affecting discretionary spending and demand for its products. Yet, its strategic manufacturing footprint and robust liquidity could possibly support its profitability, dividend longevity, and operational sustainability. This also positions ETD well for possible spillovers from the recent rate cut and decreasing mortgage rates. That is why I think its current valuation is justifiable with some upside. I still advise caution, but I believe that an optimistic outlook makes sense. Technicals are still neutral but show some buying opportunities after the recent overselling. I’m upgrading my rating on this stock from hold to buy.

data.title: Tuya: Quiet Execution, Strong Margins, And A Billion-Dollar Cash Cushion
data.description:
Tuya Inc. (NYSE:TUYA) is performing as well as I expected when I covered it in May, after Q1 2025 results came out, albeit the stock has been mostly flat. The company is staying profitable while growing, and I’m surprised at how little the market seems to care.

To be fair, it’s a Chinese tech company, and given the current macroeconomic and geopolitical environment, it does track that investors aren’t exactly flocking to buy it up. At the same time, I don’t think it’s unreasonable to start buying a company that is quietly turning into a global platform business. The customer base is huge (there are 1.5 million users on its AI Developer platform), and its AI-enabled tools are doing well in the market.

Those feed into the numbers, and revenue, margins, and cash flow are all up. As we know, if it gets big and important enough, deals can always be made to cushion the effect of tariffs and associated risks on the business.

Right now, there’s a valuation gap between how Tuya is performing and its price, and that’s why I’m staying long.

The Story Holds Up Operationally and Financially

I’ll start this section with a recap of Tuya’s Q2 2025 results. First things first: the business model is working very well. Revenue was up 9% YoY to $80.1 million, and the business’s 3 segments all trended up. We have PaaS growing 7%, SaaS and others up 16%, and Smart Solutions rising by 17%. Clearly, there’s a nice balance between all three’s contributions to the bottom line.

With gross margins, SaaS was the biggest contributor at 72%. PaaS didn’t do badly either (48.7%), and Smart Solutions came in at 22.5%. Those are nice numbers, bringing up the total gross margin to 48.4%, 40 basis points higher than the same period in 2024. Remember that all of this happened against a backdrop of tariffs and supply chain friction, so the consistency tells me that Tuya is getting its pricing and product mix right. Oh, and net profit was 3x what it was in the same period last year. The company also has a very flexible balance sheet (~$1 billion in cash helps with that), and debt is nonexistent. So, there’s a lot of opportunity to be had by reinvesting in the business.

However, the main reason I’m so confident in Tuya’s prospects is its evolution. It’s now growing beyond being an IoT platform. As I mentioned earlier, the company has over 1.5 million developers on its platform, and they’re building on top of advanced AI capabilities. During the Q2 2025 earnings call, for example, CFO Alex Yang said that “as of June 13, 2025, 93% of Tuya's shipped product categories were equipped with AI capabilities.” Along with that, the company’s AI agents now handle over 150 million interactions every day. That represents incredibly important commercial proof that Tuya is becoming a backbone for AI-driven products.

If you also look at things from an execution perspective, there’s a lot to like. The company’s Europe business has seen double-digit growth in categories like home appliances, telecoms, and real estate, which are the main drivers in APAC, and North America is seeing a lot of traction in AI pet and entertainment products. In China, e-commerce growth is the way forward. Overall, I think the business mix is a big part of why Tuya hasn’t been too bothered by tariff and currency risks, among others.

Now, none of these would add much to the story if we didn’t see the effect in the numbers. Management has managed to keep non-GAAP operating expenses lower than $40 million for the last few quarters, and they’re spending just enough to keep the AI and developer engines running while profitability is stable. I don’t think it’s common to see companies at this stage in their growth cycle manage the balance so well.

My Near-Term Outlook, And The Risks

Tuya has done pretty well across H1 2025, and I believe that the impact of the next stage of the company’s growth trajectory on the narrative largely depends on H2’s results. We already know that management still expects issues around trade friction and tariff uncertainty, and the US government’s recent moves don’t exactly inspire confidence. But we should still see steady growth and margin stability, so what I’m watching for is how the company executes through all that noise.

I expect revenue around $82-84 million for Q3 (+5-7% QoQ), and non-GAAP operating margins around the 11% mark, which is flat. The same goes for net margins and positive operating cash flow. This is in spite of the tariffs and supply chain issues, and my bullishness comes from the relative stability of the last three months. Of course, the US tariff cycle hit discretionary electronics earlier this year, and Tuya has indirect exposure. Say a manufacturer shifts production from China to Vietnam or Mexico. Tuya has to follow its customers in cases like these, and it adds short-term cost friction. Many of these countries are still talking and trying to settle tariff issues, but who absorbs the added expenses is unclear, and that can affect order timing. Again, I don’t think it will affect the company’s sales much, but I’ll keep an eye on it.

When it comes to developer engagement and AI monetization, we know that the company already has the people in its ecosystem to drive added growth and value add. What I’m looking for is more AI monetization as the next step, and that should flow through in the SaaS and PaaS numbers. I think new pricing tiers tied to AI functionality can be a real winner here, albeit Tuya needs to handle it carefully. If it can achieve higher conversion rates (developer activity to paid usage and recurring revenue), then we will begin to see the operating leverage start to really compound.

That leads me on to the company’s dollar-based net expansion rate (DBNER). This is a metric that tells us how well Tuya is managing to expand its existing customers’ use of its platform and generate more revenue from them. In Q2, that number fell to 114% from 127% in the previous period last year, mostly because of tariff-related headwinds, and I expect it to start trending up over the next few quarters slowly. That way, we can see that customers’ spending on the platform is normalizing.

Now, there are other risks, like increased competition in the space. AWS and Alibaba Cloud are huge competitors, even if they’re not as specialized as Tuya. Still, they have the advantage of size and reputation, so they might want to compete with price undercuts or better OEM relationships. It’s up to Tuya to use its developer network and platform stickiness to keep delivering what customers want, really, and that’s the one way to keep its advantage.

I’m treating Q3 and Q4 as a durability test for the company, because it doesn’t need to post breakout growth to justify a Buy for me. What we need to see is consistency, and if that happens, then there’s a lot of upside in the current valuation. We’ll talk about that next.

The Current Price Is Relatively Cheap

I don’t believe that Tuya’s valuation today matches the quality of its results. The company ended Q2 with ~$1.01 billion in net cash and investments and no meaningful debt. That translates to around $1.65 per ADS or ordinary share in cash, using a fully diluted share count of 610 million. That is a very strong balance sheet for a company of this size, especially because it’s already profitable and is a major cash generator.

If we annualize H1’s revenue (Q1+Q2) of around $155 million, we get a run-rate of $310 million. With that figure, I think it’s easy to see why the market is mispricing Tuya. Right now, its EV/Sales multiple is around 1.9x, its enterprise value is $620 million, and the market cap is $1.44 billion.

Let’s move the EV/Sales multiple up to 2.5x, and the company’s true value is much closer to $1.8 billion. If we move it up to 3x, it’s near $2 billion. Keep in mind that both assumed multiples are much lower than the sector median of 3.6x, so that’s a lot of upside currently sitting on the table.

I don’t think those are aggressive assumptions. Tuya is profitable, it has a ~48% gross margin, ~25% non-GAAP net margin, and positive operating cash flow. The business model is executing nicely, and operating expenses have been within a steady dollar range for several quarters while revenue is growing. That is consistency that gives the company the kind of financial leverage that most mid-stage platform companies don’t reach this quickly.

My Recommendation

In my view, the market discounting the stock because of tariff uncertainty and its Chinese roots is a good opportunity to get in at a good price before the dust settles. This is a business that is incredibly liquid, profitable, and paying dividends while growing steadily. At the current price, you’re paying roughly two times forward sales for a company with one of the cleanest balance sheets in the Chinese tech space and an expanding global ecosystem, and that’s a trade I’ll take anytime.

I’m confident that the business will continue its cost discipline, building recurring PaaS and SaaS revenue, and growing margins. If that happens, I see a fair bit of material upside over the next year because then, the market should have started looking at this as a sustainable buy.

data.title: VCE Over VDY: A Deep Dive Into Canada's Top Vanguard ETFs
data.description:
I believe that the Canadian Equity market represents a very interesting investment opportunity, and in this analysis, I will look at two of Vanguard ETFs aimed at this market: the Vanguard FTSE Canadian High Dividend Yield Index ETF (TSX:VDY:CA) and the Vanguard FTSE Canada Index ETF (TSX:VCE:CA). Both of them are very strong ETFs that provide investors with simple ways to get exposure to this market, with very different strategies. They are both buys, but I will give VCE a slightly better outlook against VDY. The reason behind that is that both ETFs are showing similar returns and volatility metrics, but VCE has the benefits of being more affordable, better diversified, and more representative of the broad Canadian economy. For investors that are prioritizing income above everything, VDY is an interesting option, but I believe that its high concentration on just two sectors is too much of a risk over the long term and I don’t think that the dividend premium is enough to make the fund equally as good as VCE.

The Core Strategic Divide

The difference between the two ETFs is coming from their two very different strategies. VDY is tracking the FTSE Canada High Dividend Yield Index. To build its portfolio, the index is starting from all Canadian equities in the FTSE GEIS classification and ranks companies by dividend yield. The bottom 50% is excluded and the rest of the companies are weighted by market-cap. In short, this index is composed of the highest yielding equities of the FTSE Canada index. On the other hand, VCE has a more traditional approach to equity markets. The fund is tracking the FTSE Canada Index, simply replicating the Canadian equity market without additional filters and a market-cap weighting methodology. The difference of strategy results in very different portfolios and risks, despite very close returns.

With its focus on high yield equities, the portfolio of VDY is mechanically more concentrated. The fund holds 57 companies, mainly financials (55.78%) and energy (26.64%) companies, making for more than 80% of the portfolio. If you want to invest in Canada and go with VDY, you will almost only invest in these two sectors, and the result will be a cyclical portfolio, not very representative of the broad Canadian economy. In addition, the individual holdings are also very concentrated: the top 10 account for 67.74% of the portfolio, and the top 3 alone account for 33.14% of the portfolio with two large-cap banking companies: the Royal Bank of Canada (RY:CA) at 15.17% and The Toronto-Dominion Bank (TD:CA) at 9.98%. This is of course completed by an energy company: Enbridge Inc. (ENB:CA) at 7.99%.

VCE’s profile is very different. The fund holds more companies: 79. It also has a higher degree of diversification both in terms of sectoral splits and individual holdings. Representing the Canadian economy, the financial industry still represents a significant 36.36% of the portfolio, but this is a lot less than VDY. In addition, the fund is invested in other sectors that you will not really find with VDY, as they don’t provide investors with significant income streams: Basic Materials (14.38%) and Technology (11.88%). A significant part of the tech exposure is driven by the second largest holding of the fund: Shopify Inc. (SHOP:CA), with 6.66% of the portfolio. The surge of e-commerce is a major global trend that VDY fails to capture due to its concentration on Financials and Energy, and for a broad ETF tracking a country’s performance, this is a clear miss. The individual concentration of the fund is also much lower (while still relatively high due to the market-cap weighting methodology). The top 10 holdings account for 42.16%, and the top 3 for 19.52%. I believe that the reach of VCE is clearly a strength against VDY, as the fund appears to be more robust (less cyclical), while also capturing innovative growth companies.

Below, you will find summary tables showing you sectoral splits and top 10 holdings:

Canadian Pacific Kansas City Ltd.

2.58

A Look at Risk and Reward

If we compare the performance and risk figures of the two funds, we will actually see two very similar results.

In terms of volatility, measured by the standard deviation, there is no clear winner. In the short term, VDY is slightly less volatile, but it is not the case anymore in longer time frames. VDY’s standard deviation measures are 12.14% (3 years), 13.78% (5 years), and 13.35% (10 years) against 12.46% (3 years), 12.92% (5 years), and 12.48% (10 years). If we look beyond the daily volatility and try to see what is the worst thing that happened over the last 5 years with maximum drawdowns, we can make the same conclusion that there is no clear winner and the two figures are really close: -13.17% for VDY and -13.83% for VCE.

With a purely statistical lens, the two funds are equally risked. However, the important thing to keep in mind is that these metrics are backward looking and are not to be considered predictive. If over the last 10 years, the two funds behaved similarly, there is no guarantee that they will keep doing so in the next 10 years. In addition, if I consider the extreme concentration of VDY, I actually find the above results to be very surprising and would consider that you take a risk premium into account with your assessment of VDY. VDY’s positioning makes it very sensitive to macroeconomic events, both nationally and globally, as we are dealing with large-cap companies. For example, new regulations for the banking industry of variations or energy prices would have a very strong effect on VDY, while it would be mitigated (but still present) in the portfolio of VCE.

Let’s now look at the reward part with the total returns:

Again here, there is no clear winner. In the short term, VCE has provided investors with stronger returns, while the concentrated bet of VDY has proven to be a higher source of performance for investors. However, I don’t think that these returns are coming with the same level of risks. Let’s look at Sharpe ratios, measuring the additional performance produced by an additional measure of risk taken. For VDY, it is 1.13 (3 years), 1.20 (5 years), and 0.81 (10 years) and for VCE, it is 1.27 (3 years), 1.08 (5 years), and 0.83 (10 years). As you can see, the two ratios are very close, and there is not one that is the best in all time periods. However, this measure is based on the volatility, and fails to take other risks, such as concentration into account. If I do, it makes me think that VCE is the superior fund, and the one that will outperform consistently in the long term.

The Decisive Edge

In addition to the points we mention above, the financial metrics of the two funds are very different, further strengthening the case for VCE. The first one, and a compelling one is the expense ratio. As it is only tracking the market, VCE’s expense ratio is low at 0.06%. On the other hand, VDY is almost four times more expensive with an expense ratio of 0.22%. In absolute terms, this is not a very high difference, but given how close the two funds's returns have been, this is a difference that matters, especially since VDY is more expensive, it should justify it by a strategic advantage, that I have not seen until now.

Naturally, the focus of VDY on dividends results in a portfolio with a heavier “value” tilt, as the valuation metrics are indeed showing that. The fund’s P/E ratio is 15.0 and its P/B ratio is 1.9, in opposition with VCE’s metrics: 20.5 and 2.6. This means that VCE is more priced for growth, and given the similar performances so far, you could argue that this is in favor of VDY’s portfolio. However, I only think that this is a consequence of the sectoral split, and that you should not pay attention to it too much, given that the sectoral split is a weakness of VDY, not a strength. The valuation metrics are only consistent with it.

The core appeal of VDY is its distribution yield. The fund’s dividend yield is 3.53%, as opposed to VCE’s 2.53%. I actually find the difference to be really significant considering all the trade-offs made with VDY to get this higher dividend yield. You should ask yourself if the additional 1% in dividend yield that you get with VDY is worth the concentration risk taken, especially when total returns are so close. For me, it is not worth it.

I will finish on this part with a surprising fact: VDY is a lot more popular than VCE. The fund’s AUM is $4.8 Bn against only $2.7 Bn for VCE. The fund is also a bit more liquid with an average bid/ask spread of 0.03% against 0.05% for VCE. Of course, in both cases it remains comfortable enough, but I can’t find a convincing reason why VDY is so much more popular than VCE.

Risks

While if you only look at statistical risk measures you will think that the two funds are exposed to the same risks, it actually is not the case.

The first obvious one, that I already discussed a lot, is the concentration risk, especially for VDY. 80% of the portfolio invested in only two sectors is not a country specific ETF, it is a bet on the health of Canadian banks and the price of oil: not really what you would want to get as an income investor looking for low-risk investments. This exposes you to significant risks of sector specific shocks, and is further amplified by the single stock concentration of the fund. Of course, VCE is not perfect on this aspect and is also a concentrated fund, but compared to VDY, it is clear that the risk taken is much lower.

In addition, relying too much on two sectors introduces opportunity risks, filtering out sectors that are not paying high dividends. I have in mind the 0.74% allocation to tech, which is a real miss as it is a strong growth engine for the country with worldwide companies whose services are used everywhere like Shopify. The current market environment is still driven by tech, and getting no exposure to it as a country specific ETF is surprising.

In terms of sectors, VCE is also exposed to risks, but it is more about systematic market risk than it is about sector specific risk. And quite frankly, when investing in a country’s economy, there is no way that you avoid this risk. What you can do is make sure that your portfolio is well diversified so that you will suffer less from a broad market downturn, and while the figures of maximum drawdown are very close, I think that VCE is better equipped to navigate all kinds of shocks.

Conclusion

Both ETFs are strong options, and I rate them buys because they are so similar and, in their own way, provide investors with similar returns. However, I do think that VCE is a slightly better fund because it doesn’t carry the strong concentration risk of VDY (which can be an interesting feature for some), is significantly cheaper, and better representative of the Canadian Economy as a whole.

data.title: The Cooper Companies: Defensive Gem With Insider Buying And Building Momentum
data.description:
For months I have been focusing on defensive buy ideas. Another pick popping on my momentum screens this week is The Cooper Companies, Inc. (NASDAQ:COO). Cooper is a healthcare and medical products firm, manufacturing and selling contact lenses as its primary business (with the most contact wearers in the global marketplace at 43 million), plus major fertility and surgical divisions.

The company is growing at a steady pace (although at a slow rate). Cooper holds a rare tangible book value balance sheet in the medical products industry (around $14 per share), with very manageable net debt of $2.35 billion (vs. operating cash flow of $816 million over the past four quarters).

Four ideas stand out as the bull arguments in October 2025. First, looking at enterprise valuations of the operating business, Cooper is sitting at a decade-low position for new investment. Second, insiders have been heavy buyers of the stock since early September. Third, underlying trading momentum has actually been quite positive over the past several months. Fourth, COO has an investment history of outperforming the S&P 500 during recessions (I personally believe we are very close to a recession going into 2026).

Adding all four variables together, COO jumps out as a unique and credible purchase for long-term accounts. While many Big Tech stocks are trading at huge overvaluations, why not focus on finding value in the medical products sector, after a share price decline over a number of years?

If we do slip into recession and get a major turn lower in the U.S. equity market during 2026, I expect Cooper to be a solid outperformance winner vs. the S&P 500. Intelligent investors willing to grab an attractive set of value, growth, and defensive investment characteristics should be reviewing the Cooper upside story. I am initiating a Buy rating for Cooper, using a 12-month outlook for the stock.

The Business

A good overall review of the business is part of the latest August 2025 Investor Presentation here.

Revenue is running around $4 billion on a trailing basis, with close to $4 in non-GAAP cash EPS. Both are expected to rise slightly by the end of 2025. The positive operating business readout is that Cooper is a leader in contact lenses globally and runs a diversified list of fertility and surgical products as complementary units.

Positive Valuation Picture

The foundational driver for my investment interest is the low and sliding enterprise valuation setup. EV (which includes debt + equity) to core EBITDA (13.3x forward estimates) and sales (4.04x forward) have been trading at 10-year lows since summer. You have to go back to 2013 to find a lower valuation setup. Both numbers are now well below long-term averages back to 1990.

The forward 13.3x ratio of EV to EBITDA is considerably below the 20x multiple of three years ago, and sits in the middle of the pack vs. related peers/competitors in eye healthcare and medical products today.

The forward P/E estimate of 16.8x is well off the 31x number of July 2023, and trending toward the lower end of my peer sort group.

Free cash flow yield of almost 3% does not really grab your attention as a value play, but is in the middle of the peer group. I believe it could also be increased to 4% or even 5%, if the company slashed its relatively high (for the industry) capital expenditures.

Insiders Turning Bullish

Management has flipped from sellers of the stock in the $90s for price earlier in the year to buyers in the $60s during September.

The purchases started after Q3 FY2025 earnings were released, as the stock sold off on mildly disappointing GAAP EPS and lowered sales guidance for the second half of the year. My assumption is management is more optimistic about 2026, perhaps believing today's price and valuation are too low (representing something of a bargain vs. historical comparisons).

Attractive Chart Pattern (Bottoming)

Perhaps the primary reason for optimism over Cooper's immediate share price outlook is the momentum pattern since April. At that point, many indicators of stock trading health turned higher. I have circled in green below the bullish turns in the Accumulation/Distribution Line, Negative Volume Index, and On Balance Volume.

Despite the stock quote falling to new lows in August, a bullish divergence in these (and other) momentum signals could soon support a rising price trend. For sure, the large volume turnover since late May means shares have been exchanged between weak-hand holders (investors giving up on the price drop) to strong-hand owners (those looking for value and defensive characteristics over a longer period of time). I believe the background technical picture is actually quite strong to support price in the $60s.

Trading History During Recessions

The 2020 COVID pandemic, 2007-09 Great Financial Crisis, 2000-02 dot-com technology bust/recession, and 1990-91 Persian Gulf War economic contraction are the four episodes of sliding GDP growth since Cooper began public trading in 1986.

1990-91

The Persian Gulf War situation provided the first test of Cooper's defensive trading characteristics. Wall Street history tends to favor consumer staples and medical-related stocks during stress events in the economy. The thinking goes sales and demand will remain relatively stable for these firms, while consumers may cut back on travel, auto and home purchases, dining out, etc., if they are worried about losing their job (or actually become unemployed). You can review the meaningful outperformance gains achieved by Cooper during 1990 and early 1991 below.

2000-05

The next recession occurred on the original technology bust of 2000-02, which could be analogous to the AI-inspired Big Tech boom since 2023. Cooper performed quite well as the U.S. stock market peaked in 2000, while strong gains for stocks would follow for a stretch of years into late 2004.

2007-09

Between the S&P 500 peak of late 2007, through the worst months of the Great Recession, and following the recovery from economic turmoil into 2010, Cooper performed closer to in-line vs. the major U.S. market averages. The second half of 2010 did witness some extra gains by COO. So, long-term investors would have benefited from owning the stock during this period in history.

2020

Again, during the 2020 COVID pandemic shutdown, Cooper performed at a rate close to the S&P 500, although owning Big Tech would have been a more productive proposition coming out of the worst fears for the economy since 2009.

Overall, two recessionary periods proved a great time to own Cooper Companies, while two produced market-similar returns. The net average conclusion of all four is it would have been smarter to own COO during and slightly after the recession, than most other businesses.

Final Thoughts

Unfortunately, Cooper does not pay a dividend check quarterly. Instead, the company has decided to focus on share buybacks for the return of investor capital.

In this vein, management just upped its share repurchase program by another $1 billion (on a current equity market capitalization of $14 billion). With free cash flow in the $400 to $500 million range annually (going forward as my estimate), the company could easily retire 3% to 4% of outstanding counts each of the next two years, without taking on new debt.

When you combine this repurchase effort with the fact that Cooper is still spending a decent amount on CAPEX ($400+ million annually) for product improvements and bringing new ones to market, current Wall Street analyst estimates may prove on the low side of reality a few years out. In a recession, 10% growth rates for both "per share" income and sales would be a terrific result (in my view).

What could go wrong and lead to investment losses in Cooper? I would say a sharp market implosion (like a 1987-style crash on Wall Street or black-swan event like the 2020 pandemic) may prove too much for COO, even with its lower valuation profile.

In addition, if a deep recession hits in 2026, the valuation could move even lower, similar to how things played out during 2008-09. Yet, any oversized drop in COO should open up an even smarter area for long-term accumulation.

Cooper Companies represents yet another selection I have added to the defensive side of my portfolio. Since the summertime, I have talked about a variety of smaller medical product names and food makers as the areas I am concentrating my buying.

If we are on the verge of a 20% bear market or something worse in the 40% to 50% decline range from a serious recession, you will want to hold a long list of defensive ideas. Flight-to-safety money flows could easily reverse Cooper's downtrend in price during late 2025 with little warning.

Assuming history repeats, COO could soon morph into a solid outperformance selection (hedge to a degree) if we get a slow-grinding recession and bear market span like 2000-02 or 2007-09.

I rate Cooper Companies shares a Buy for a 12-month outlook. My forecast is COO will outperform the S&P 500 over the next year, even if small losses appear (measured against the potential for unusually large S&P 500 losses on one of the greatest market overvaluations ever on price to sales, total market cap to GDP, the 10-year CAPE Ratio).

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: U.S.-China Trade Tensions: From Complacency To Concern
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

The latest

Over recent weeks, tensions between the United States and China have intensified, culminating in the U.S. threatening to impose an additional 100% tariff on Chinese imports starting November 1, alongside expanded controls on critical software exports.

Markets initially responded with a sharp risk-off shift, but a more measured tone from President Trump and China’s apparent reluctance to escalate further—despite intermittent flare-ups—helped restore a degree of calm. Nonetheless, this episode serves as a stark reminder of the latent risks in trade relations, an area that had faded from the macro spotlight, and has jolted market complacency.

Background

U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods had stabilized at approximately 54%, comprising a 30% increase atop pre-existing 24% levies. However, in late September, the U.S. announced a significant expansion of its export control regime, with major implications for Chinese firms’ access to U.S. technologies.

In response, China declared a substantial expansion of its own export controls, particularly targeting rare earth minerals—a critical input for AI development. Notably, China dominates this space, controlling over 90% of global supply. Given the concentration of tech in U.S. equity markets and the pivotal role of AI-related capex in supporting U.S. growth, this move poses a material threat to both the U.S. economy and markets.

Conversely, China remains heavily reliant on U.S. chipmaking equipment, and Beijing’s ongoing economic fragility suggests it too is ill-positioned for a prolonged trade war.

Forward-looking implications

Tariffs had largely receded from macro strategy discussions, owing to perceived stability and limited economic impact. However, the proposed 100% increased tariff would have significant potential impacts moving forward.

Tariff impact: A 100% levy would raise the effective China tariff to ~155%, lifting the U.S. average effective tariff rate from ~13% to nearly 30%—the highest in over a century, even surpassing post–Liberation Day levels.

Economic hit: Preliminary estimates suggest a ~3% drag on U.S. GDP and inflation breaching 4%, well above the Fed’s 2% target. Even if treated as transitory, the magnitude would likely compel a more cautious Fed stance.

Trade embargo risk: Such a steep tariff could trigger a de facto trade embargo. Following the April announcement of a 145% tariff, container traffic from China to the U.S. collapsed, raising fears of supply chain disruptions and empty shelves.

China’s vulnerability: While the U.S. would suffer, China’s export-dependent economy would also face significant strain, particularly amid domestic economic weakness.

Fiscal repercussions: A breakdown in trade could severely dent U.S. tariff revenues, potentially refocusing investor attention on fiscal deficits and exerting upward pressure on long-end Treasury yields.

Strategic outlook

The severity of a renewed trade war increases the probability of eventual compromise. However, a return to the previous status quo appears unlikely. President Trump may be recognizing China’s leverage in rare earths, potentially prompting a more conciliatory approach to re-engage negotiations.

The path to compromise is expected to be volatile. Further escalation remains possible, and even post-resolution, tariffs and non-tariff measures (such as export controls and investment restrictions) are poised to remain a key geopolitical lever, especially as the U.S. and China continue their strategic rivalry in AI and technology. Trade frictions are likely to persist as a feature of the global macro landscape.

Market outlook

Renewed tensions have exposed market fragility, particularly given stretched valuations and elevated sensitivity to growth shocks. Investor complacency around tariffs has been disrupted, prompting a reassessment of risk.

While a compromise is anticipated, markets may remain jittery in the near term. End-of-cycle concerns are mounting, and the government shutdown has created a data vacuum that amplifies uncertainty. Still, the broader macro backdrop remains constructive: resilient growth, underpinned by policy support (monetary and fiscal) and sustained transformative technology investment, suggests markets can withstand setbacks. Distinguishing between idiosyncratic and systemic shocks is crucial.

In this uncertain environment, a balanced, diversified approach remains essential—staying constructive on risk assets where fundamentals warrant, while managing valuation risks and maintaining portfolio resilience.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Middlesex Water: A Very Split Thesis, But With Upside
data.description:
In this article, I'll provide an update on the Middlesex Water Company (MSEX).

Middlesex is one of the water-based or water-oriented (business-wise) companies that I cover on Seeking Alpha. Other companies in the similar sector include York Water (YORW) as well as European companies either in water utilities, like Veolia (OTCPK:VEOEY), or industrial water infrastructure. My last article on Middlesex can be found here.

My previous article was over 4 months back, and my thesis at the time was a positive one. Because it's been almost half a year at this point, because the company has come up with new results, and because I believe the time has come to update the thesis (also a new PT for the company), I believe it's a good time for an update here.

When I last covered it, the valuation for the company had normalized from premium levels. While this company isn't exactly a "yield king" with a sub-4% dividend yield, water companies typically tend toward lower yields, and this is not an argument that makes the company uninvestable.

Instead, the business model, which includes regulated income similar to other utilities, is what creates the appeal for investing here. And in today's volatile world, there's definitely something to be said for investing in something safe and slow-growing but with the defensive nature that's not going anywhere - such as water, heat, electricity, food, or the like. Things that, regardless of societal change (which seems significant at this time), are going to remain mostly "as is".

Middlesex isn't the only company here, but it's one of the better ones.

Why?

Let me show you and use this article as a preview for the 3Q25 period, which is upcoming soon - also this update is relevant due to the recent (July) rate case decision, effective as of the 3rd of July this year.

Middlesex Water Company - Valuation Appeal And Upside Exist Here

Collecting it, treating it, distributing it, and also selling it, that's what Middlesex does with water. It's not just residential or commercial, but also industrial sales of water, and also specialized business areas, such as fire protection. The company's history includes growth from a single water utility to, over time, an enterprise with operating units. It's bigger than YORW, for instance.

While it does have commercial and industrial customers, the vast majority of its customer exposure remains residential. A big part of these companies is what sort of relationships they have with the regulator, because regulators are the deciding factor when it comes to how much these companies are "allowed" to earn. Pricing is typically set, and allowed to rise depending on maintenance and expansion needs, as well as an "allowed" profit or return, usually ROE. The company has been a dividend king for 2 years, meaning it's paid uninterrupted dividend increases for over 50 years. This is one of the few companies in the world, and in the US market, that has done this.

Furthermore, its fundamentals are cemented by an A rating from S&P Global, again not atypical for a water company; YORW also has this, but it's definitely good.

One of the things investors should pay attention to is the fact that the company doesn't just have regulated operations. This is one of the advantages that many of these utilities have started to include. The inclusion of non-regulated operations creates, theoretically, an economic buffer for when earnings from the regulated businesses aren't as good. Also, again theoretically, they offer a good use of excess cash for expansion and investing. Still, this company has a vast majority of regulated business operations.

The name of the game for MSEX, just as with many of these utilities, is infrastructure investment to get rate base growth. The current 6-year rate base growth averages 12.1% as of mid-2025 and the 2Q25 results, which is good.

It's definitely worth mentioning here that MSEX is expecting a heavier investment period upcoming. That means that EPS is going to be lower in terms of growth (usually flat, or 1-3% growth, as evidenced by recent forecasts for 2025E, which we'll go into in the valuation section), but it also obviously means infrastructure safety for the future. The last higher period of infrastructure investment was during the late 2010s and early 2020s, and the company's annual infrastructure investment is set to rise above $100M for the first time in over 5 years next fiscal, with a current estimate of $133M. For the next 3 years in total, almost $400M is expected, but this includes resilience and preventive investments as well, including a large $100M+ investment in a PFAS treatment plant, the company's RENEW program, replacement programs, and the like. In short, a lot of different things are set to make the company more attractive on a forward basis.

The recent base rate case decision from the regulator was a positive one. It allows an RoE of 9.5% at an equity ratio of 53.5%. The allowed RoE is determined with the regulator looking at things like capital structure, cost of debt, as well as other expenses. RoE represents the profit margin that is then compared to other enterprises with corresponding risk profiles, while at the same time allowing the company to survive (but not only that) and to attract capital. It's fair to say that the RoE in a rate case decision is key to what revenue we can expect, and then, going by the company's typical profit margin, we can see where it goes.

In New Jersey, the company has gone even higher in terms of what it wants. The rate case was filed on June 30th, effective in less than a year from now, where the company has requested an RoE of 10.5% - my expectation is between 9.5-10% here based on previous numbers. The company's requests represent a revenue increase request of almost $25M at a rate base of close to $650M.

During the second quarter, which is the last quarter we have to look at here, the company grew its revenues by less than 0.5%, but at least we saw growth. Net income and earnings were up by low single digits, below 2.5%, but again, positive for this sort of company.

However, I fully expect things to decline from here on out. The reason for this expectation is the company's extensive 3-year investment plan, which, as we're speaking and considering the company, is already ongoing. Expect, therefore, lower EPS for 2025. 2026 might be a year of growth, but it will largely depend on how exactly the rate case mentioned above turns out. Costs and pressures are heavy everywhere, and regulators are in a position where they have to both look at what is reasonable to expect customers to pay, while at the same time taking into consideration that the company needs to survive and be attractive to operate as it does. Not an enviable position to be in. Which side "wins out" depends on the situation, the case, and the geography.

The best thing MSEX can do to guarantee as good returns as possible is to have as little debt as possible (it's below 47.5% at this time), with maturities of less than $10M per year, and dropping to less than $8M in 2027E and forward. So on that basis, I'd say we're well-covered.

The above being said, I will mention that when it comes to MSEX and water utilities overall, water is still a cheap expense in the US (for the most part). For MSEX average customers, their water bill is less than 1% of their household income.

2Q25 was as expected - but I expect 3Q25 to be a bit more "telling" with regard to the increased investment requirements, meaning we might see a bit more of a downside here.

Valuation for Middlesex - The upside is still conservatively over 20% per year at this price.

In my last article, I made it clear that the company constitutes a SWAN investment, and that doesn't change here. The trading below 25x average P/E (slightly above the 24x P/E average of my last article), still puts the company both below peers, as well as, significantly below the average P/E for the shorter and medium term. The only argument to a significant downside here is if you were to argue that the company's 3-year investment plan represents a massive downside and an argument to value the company much, much lower than we've seen in the past.

I do not believe such an argument makes sense.

My last PT for the company was $62/share. That means buy below $62/share - and I believe the company has a fair value of close to $80/share, based on a forward estimate of around 28-29x P/E for the 2026-2027E period. In a bearish scenario, I would drop this to $75/share, provided that the rate case outcome in NJ is worse than expected. If the company gets a better result than expected (such as getting its requested RoE), I would raise this to $85/share. That's the range we're "moving in" here and the possibilities that I see. Based on the company's fundamentals, its typical earnings accuracy over time, and peer valuations, I consider this appealing enough for a continued "BUY". Because I forecast the 3Q25 period to potentially be below expectations, I believe a case can be made here to wait for those results prior to investing.

Peers are also appealing, is the thing. York Water comes to mind. The company has dropped to a 22.5x P/E, which is very low for this business. On a 5-year average, the upside for the company looks as follows until 2026E.

Even if we were to value the company at the same 29-30x P/E as MSEX, the upside is still over 40% on an annual basis inclusive of the dividend, with an implied FV of close to $50/share.

This means that while Middlesex is a "BUY", I believe YORW to be more attractive and the "BUY" I would consider at this time.

My risk considerations for the MSEX thesis are as follows.

Risks To My Thesis

Looking at the future, as mentioned, the one risk I see is that the combination of increased servicing and infrastructure costs, coupled with lower returns, would justify a fundamentally lower premium for water companies. This remains the risk going into 3Q25, and I believe the current forecast for the company's earnings confirms this. This risk is similar to YORW, but YORW is cheaper, which makes it, in turn, more attractive.

This is a risk I consider worth taking. If you're either, like me, fully invested in Veolia or similar companies or want a non-international stock, then what you have here is a good, safe upside and what I view to be a SWAN.

My thesis for the SWAN is as follows.

Thesis

Middlesex is a solid dividend king among other water companies, which share the company's fundamental qualities and upside. The positives for these companies include a safe dividend, safe operations usually unaffected by external turmoil due to the necessary qualities of their operations. Their risks are typically limited to local environmental disasters/effects, and the need for retrofitting or addressing their service infrastructure. Risks in this particular company are low, even if I view YORW as being a somewhat better and safer investment overall.

I give the company a PT of $62/share, unchanged from my previous article, going into 3Q25.

I therefore view Middlesex Water as a "BUY" and would consider opening a position in the company for the long term. I believe we have an annualized upside of at least 20% or higher here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (bolded).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $62/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2025.

data.title: VGUS: Parking Spot For Cash
data.description:
Introduction

The Vanguard Ultra-Short Treasury ETF (NASDAQ:VGUS) is an ETF that allows you to park excess liquidity with a yield higher than that of a checking account or a traditional money fund. Its investment objective is to follow an index of US Treasuries, with maturities of less than 12 months, which means that the fund assumes a duration of 0.4 years and consequently a risk of almost non-existent duration. Its current expense ratio is 0.07% and is a fabulous vehicle to park credit on government debt, without looking for complications in corporate or mortgage tranches.

The index is monitored or replicated by the management team using the representative sampling technique. The Bloomberg Short Treasury Index remains a very clear and narrow index, as it has only Treasury bonds with maturities of less than 12 months. As a market-weighted index, the weight of each issue depends on the size of the outstanding balance; and because everything matures soon, the portfolio naturally rotates into new auctions and reissues.

All these features place the ETF at the shortest part of the interest rate curve. This implies that the VGUS investor will have a lower volatility investing here, than in funds with a longer duration. It will have yields that are fairly aligned with current official rates, owing to the relationship between interest rates and the price of money.

An important point in any analysis is to understand how an ETF makes money. The main source of return in this case is the interest earned on the treasury bonds held by the fund (carry), which is distributed as a monthly dividend. With everything less than 12 months away, the price risk from rate movements is small but not non-existent. In rate-increasing environments, NAV may fall somewhat (little, for a short duration), in downward environments, the price may rise slightly.

A relevant point that the reader may question is the difference between buying a money fund or a very short-term T-Bill ETF. Here every alternative has its place. In addition to T-Bills, MMFs often invest in repos and other very short-term debt instruments. While VGUS being an ETF is trading during the day and can be more comfortable to assign to a portfolio of ETFs. In contrast to a 0–3 month ETF (such as VBIL), VGUS extends the spectrum slightly up to 12 months, which smoothens rotation and at certain times can capture an additional premium if the 1–12 month slope is positive.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyze some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

The first relevant metric to analyze this ETF is the front-end carry (1-3M), compared to its slope and the interest rate on reserves balance (IORB). The main line (yield_nowcast) is the yield nowcast that the ETF can capture when it rotates its Treasury bills.

To calculate it, we take the average of the 1 and 3 months to approximate the “sweet spot” where the portfolio usually lives (all below 12 months, with the WAM very short). On that axis we add the slope of the 1-3M section, which indicates when the short section is slightly inverted and limits the additional carry. The second element is the difference between headroom (yield nowcast) and the IORB rate. In a situation where this headroom is positive, the bills offer a slightly higher return than the market “floor” (IORB rate).

Today, we can see that the nowcast of the fund has cooled, from the peak zone of the years 2023-2024 and has been stabilizing in an area near the IORB. On the other hand, the green line (headroom) is located in levels very close to zero. This situation is typical of market phases where haircuts are discounted or “extra” premium levels are required on the 1-3M tranche of bills. With this reading, we can draw several conclusions in this first metric:

  • The first is that today the fund continues to pass on the official rate pulse to its dividend, but with little “extra” margin above ground.
  • Secondly, the slope of tranche 1-3M with its flattening, suggests that the roll of that tranche is not adding hardly any carry, so that the profitability of the fund will follow very closely the evolution of the official rates.

In the second metric, the idea we have is to control the cash market. We do this by calculating a composite indicator consisting of three variables (the bank reserves deposited with the EDF, the levels of IRRs, and the AMT account). Each series is normalized with z-scores to compare different magnitudes on a common scale (higher z implies above its historical mean, lower z implies below). With these elements, the variable LIQ_z (which is equal to z(reserves) − z(RRP) − z(TGA) is assembled, and attempts to capture the “useful” level of liquidity for a money market, where more reserves tend to favor the circulation of cash, while a widely used RRP and a bulky TGA tend to absorb (the Treasury keeps more money in its account, and that money is not in the loop).

In addition, variations at 4 weeks of each component (Δ4w) are added to see the recent rate of change, because many times the turn of conditions comes by the weekly dynamics before it is seen at the absolute levels. With this panel, we get a “market fund” reading for the 0–12 month tranche where the ETF operates (a loose environment usually accompanies smooth auctions and orderly spreads; a more tense environment usually leads to longer queues and some friction in financing).

Reading the current graph, we show that the liquidity level (LIQ_z) moves close to zero, with certain impulses that do not consolidate over time. When the Δ4w of the RRP is negative and reserves do not show continuous drops, the bill market tends to operate normally, and the return on new issues aligns with the official rates, which for an ETF like VGUS translates into consistent monthly dividends and very contained price volatility. In those segments where the TGA accumulates tickets for several weeks, the circuit becomes more demanding (more competition for cash available, somewhat tighter auctions, less “space” above the rate floor).

Conclusion

As I see the current situation, I recommend keeping the analyzed vehicle in your wallet, if you already have it. As we have seen, the extra carry that could squeeze the ETF, is very limited at the moment. It is normal to assume a gradual decline in monthly distributions if the market consolidates expectations of cuts. In addition to this, we have the liquidity indicator that marks us a neutral point at the moment, which means that bills do not pay a large premium with respect to the market floor (IORB). Thank you for reading.

data.title: TEI: Inconsistent Dividend Coverage Limits Appeal
data.description:
Overview

Templeton Emerging Markets Income Fund (NYSE:TEI) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns from its portfolio of global income securities. An income fund with a focus on securities outside of the US can be great for investors looking to diversify their income streams away from the reliance on US markets, especially as indexes continue to hover near their all-time highs. Additionally, TEI can be a great way to hedge against the decline of the US Dollar.

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that TEI's share price has increased by about 7.6%. The fund has been able to efficiently participate in the market rally over the last few months. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 19.1% over the same time frame. The main appeal of TEI is the large starting dividend yield of 9.1% and monthly payouts. This makes the fund an enticing choice for investors seeking a high level of income generation within their portfolio.

While I do believe that maintaining some global exposure within a portfolio can be an efficient hedge, there are some underlying operating flaws that limit the appeal of this fund. For instance, I believe that the dividend policy is too generous at this time, and there are several instances where the fund is paying out more than it actually earns. Similarly, the fund's NAV growth has remained relatively poor in recent years. Despite the fund's focus on securities outside of the US, TEI has demonstrated a vulnerability to the higher interest rate environment. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that TEI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, TEI has total net assets of $318M that are allocated across 128 different holdings. Under normal market conditions, the fund aims to allocate at least 80% of its assets into income-producing securities of sovereign entities, which can include private companies in emerging markets. In simpler language, TEI essentially invests in different debt securities on a global scale.

Looking at the portfolio's breakdown by region, we can see that TEI is most heavily focused on South Africa at 16.04% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Egypt and Kazakhstan, which accounts for 13.90% and 6.79% of assets, respectively. In fact, nearly 64% of the assets are allocated to regions across the Middle East and Africa. The second-largest portion of the fund is allocated across different regions in Latin America and the Caribbean at a little over 26% of assets. Approximately 3.18% of the fund's assets are allocated to North American securities, which truly makes this a global fund.

Since the fund is allocated to emerging markets, TEI is primarily allocated to securities with a lower credit rating. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB are considered to be below investment grade. These are typically borrowers with less consistent cash flows or that may be more reliant on debt to fuel operations. The lower credit rating implies a higher level of risk of losses. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 26.96%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 87.77%

Valuation

Since TEI is structured as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, TEI currently trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 7%. For reference, TEI has traded at an average discount to NAV of about 8.8% over the last five years. To better visualize the current discount pricing in relation to its historical range, I added the red line to the chart below. As we can see, TEI currently trades at the higher end of its price to NAV range over the last decade. However, this pricing position doesn't necessarily indicate whether or not it's a good time to accumulate shares.

To get better clarity of its internal performance, I reviewed TEI's updated semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.33 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.67 per share. Assuming similar performance over the remainder of 2025, TEI is aligned to generate net investment income that is on par with prior years. While net investment income levels are consistent, they do not cover the distribution in entirety. This means that TEI is partially reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings.

The challenge is that net realized gains are dependent on positive market momentum, which allows TEI to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Therefore, it can be hard to predict what TEI's earnings will look like through different market cycles. For instance, TEI still suffered a large net realized loss of $1.13 per share in 2021, despite market indexes running higher. This inconsistency in earnings has ultimately contributed to TEI's stunted NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $8.92 per share
  • 2021: $7.58 per share
  • 2022: $5.72 per share
  • 2023: $5.90 per share
  • 2024: $5.65 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $6.36 per share

As the US Dollar Index (DXY) continues to trend down, TEI can be an efficient way to offset this. For reference, the US dollar index has declined by nearly 13% over the last five years, with most of that decline taking place in 2025. Additionally, other sources of uncertainty related to tariffs, lower interest rates, and the continued US government shutdown can also have a negative impact on the US Dollar.

Risk

Since TEI's portfolio is comprised of different debt securities with poor credit ratings, the fund is exposed to the risk of defaults. While there is a lower chance that governments suffer through a default, it still doesn't entirely eliminate the possibility. S&P Global compiled data of all the defaults on a global scale. The data indicates that defaults are slowing for 2025, which may be attributed to a lower trending federal funds rate. However, it's important to note that even though defaults are trending lower, they still remain on the higher end of their range on a global scale.

However, I still remain cautious here despite the Fed recently initiating a 25 basis point cut to rates. The federal funds rate still remains within range of its ten-year highs, which directly impacts the cost of debt for borrowers. A higher cost of debt translates to a higher amount of interest rate borrowers need to pay over time, which can put pressure on TEI's portfolio and limit growth potential. Therefore, I believe that higher rates will continue to pose a threat to TEI's NAV growth, as we can see in the chart below. TEI has struggled to see its share price climb back to its pre-pandemic levels more than five years ago.

While optimistic investors may see this as an attractive time to accumulate while rates are high, there's always a risk that we remain in a high interest rate environment for a prolonged period of time. It's still too early to determine how low rates will fall, especially if the Fed decides to keep the next few cuts small as well. We still have a long way to go before rates are back near their pre-pandemic levels.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0475 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.1%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.57 per share. Now that the uncertainty of the pandemic is over, I anticipate TEI to produce earnings that exceed payouts more consistently. If we look back over the last few years, we can see that the fund has faced challenges at producing a sufficient level of earnings only a few years ago. For reference, here are the annual earnings:

  • 2020: ($0.46) per share
  • 2021: ($0.58) per share
  • 2022: ($1.22) per share
  • 2023: $0.75 per share
  • 2024: $0.32 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $1.00 per share

As we can see, TEI has only been able to generate a sufficient level of income to support payouts in two of the last six periods, if we include 2025's semi-annual results. As a result, we can see how the dividend payouts have trending down over the last five years below. While payouts have remained fairly consistent in recent years, I still remain a bit cautious going forward. There's still uncertainty around the debt markets, which can cause the fund to be impacted by declines.

Additionally, it's worth highlighting that the distributions received from TEI will not be tax-efficient. According to the latest section19(a) notice, the majority of the payouts were funded by net investment income, which are typically classified as ordinary dividends and have the greatest tax burden for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in TEI would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that would like to limit their tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that TEI is only a hold for now. Although the share price has remained suppressed in recent years, the fund doesn't trade at a large discount to NAV valuation. In addition, the NAV growth has remained limited in this higher interest rate environment. As interest rates remain elevated, there's always the chance of debt markets deteriorating and TEI's share price declining. Even though the fund offers a high dividend yield, the track record of consistent payouts is questionable.

data.title: Policy Risks A Distant Second To The Real Economy
data.description:
By Russ Koesterich, CFA, JD

In this article, Russ Koesterich discusses the resilience in the U.S. economy and why he believes stocks will continue their climb higher through year-end.

The list of policy-related risks is long, varied and growing, including lingering tariff uncertainty, a government shutdown with the potential to exacerbate an already soft labor market, and rising geopolitical tension in Europe. And of course, these risks are occurring against the backdrop of a stock market that is not exactly cheap.

The collective response of equity investors? Bid stocks to record highs. While more bearish investors can rightly point to high valuations and unpriced tail risks, a rising market is not as irrational as it might seem.

Three factors support investors downplaying policy risks. The economy and earnings are solid; rates are moving lower amidst a collapse of volatility; investors have generally been paid to fade geopolitical and policy risks.

Returning to Trend

One of the main reasons investors are looking past the tariffs and government shutdown is the ongoing resilience in the U.S. economy. Last spring, at the peak of tariff uncertainty, economic expectations collapsed. At that time, the average expected 2025 GDP forecast of a Bloomberg survey of economists fell from 2.3% in February to 1.4% in May.

It has subsequently rebounded to 1.8%, a level broadly consistent with trend growth. And as economic expectations have risen, so have earnings expectations. After plunging in the first half of the year, analysts now expect better than 12% growth for S&P 500 earnings over next year.

Calmer Waters

Recently, investors have derived further support from the bond market. 10-year bond yields are approximately 0.50% below the spring peak, with the decrease mostly a function of lower real rates, indicating easier financial conditions.

Not only are yields lower, but markets are calmer. The MOVE Index, which measures volatility in the Treasury market, is roughly 50% below its spring peak and at the lowest level since early 2022 (see Chart 1).

U.S Treasury Volatility

Source: LSEG Datastream, Merrill Lynch and BlackRock Investment Institute. Oct 01, 2025

Note: MOVE Index is a measure of implied volatility on 1-month U.S. Treasury options.

While headlines may rattle markets, during the past several decades, investors have been conditioned to fade geopolitical risk, from North Korean missile launches to government shutdowns. In most instances, buying any dip proved a profitable strategy. It is also helpful that few of the economic transmission mechanisms that can accompany a potential crisis, such as higher oil prices, are currently an issue.

What the market would not look through

None of which is to suggest that investors will or should ignore any shock. What events would have a higher likelihood of derailing the market rally? Three potential flags to watch: geopolitical tension leading to higher oil prices, tariffs causing a more meaningful spike in inflation and/or a prolonged government shutdown that materially weakens the labor market.

So far, my base case is oil prices stay low, inflation while still elevated is manageable and the shutdown is unlikely to derail the economy. Until proven otherwise, the path of least resistance for stocks in Q4 is higher.

Russ Koesterich, CFA, is a Portfolio Manager for BlackRock's Global Allocation Fund and Lead Portfolio Manager for BlackRock’s Global Allocation (GA) Selects Model Portfolios and is a regular contributor to Market Insights.

Investing involves risk, including possible loss of principal.

Commodities' prices may be highly volatile. Prices may be affected by various economic, financial, social and political factors, which may be unpredictable and may have a significant impact on the prices of precious metals. Concentrated investments in specific industries, sectors, markets or asset classes may underperform or be more volatile than other industries, sectors, markets or asset classes and the general securities market. A significant portion of the aggregate world gold holdings is owned by governments, central banks and related institutions. One or more of these institutions could sell in amounts large enough to cause a decline in world gold prices. Should there be an increase in the level of hedge activity of gold producing companies, it could cause a decline in world gold prices. Should the speculative community take a negative view towards gold, it could cause a decline in world gold prices.

This material is not intended to be relied upon as a forecast, research or investment advice, and is not a recommendation, offer or solicitation to buy or sell any securities or to adopt any investment strategy. The opinions expressed are as of October 2025 and may change as subsequent conditions vary. The information and opinions contained in this post are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy. As such, no warranty of accuracy or reliability is given and no responsibility arising in any other way for errors and omissions (including responsibility to any person by reason of negligence) is accepted by BlackRock, its officers, employees or agents. This post may contain “forward-looking” information that is not purely historical in nature. Such information may include, among other things, projections and forecasts. There is no guarantee that any forecasts made will come to pass. Reliance upon information in this post is at the sole discretion of the reader.
